raw_consumer_statement:
I am reporting unauthorized charges on my American Express account and requesting a full investigation into how my personal and financial data was accessed and used possibly through internal misuse by someone at American Express or a connected merchant or processor. 

American Express first flagged a suspicious charge, which I confirmed as fraudulent. After logging into my account, I found additional unauthorized purchases that had not been flagged. I immediately contacted AmEx to report them. My card was not lost or stolen it remained in my possession at all times, and I did not authorize anyone else to use it. 

I requested that the card be canceled and a replacement issued. While AmEx ultimately reversed the charges, the process required numerous calls and delays, and I was left frustrated by the lack of transparency and coordination. 

More troubling is that I received shipment notifications to my personal email address the same email linked to my AmEx account showing that the fraudulent purchases were being shipped to a real physical address in another state. Specifically : XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXXXXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX, CA XXXX United States This address is not mine, nor do I have any connection to it. It is a modified version of my actual billing address, likely used to pass fraud checks while rerouting the goods. The use of my correct email, full name, and partial address, along with the fact that I received live shipment updates, strongly suggests that my account information was pulled directly not guessed or skimmed. 

This does not appear to be ordinary external fraud. It shows signs of insider access or collusion, especially given my long-standing relationship with some of the retailers involved. It raises serious questions about whether an AmEx employee or merchant-side actor had access to and misused my data.
